text: Hello, I'm sharing another robotic proctopexy for full thickness rectal prolapse. This was a patient who had had prolapse for several years and finally was interested in repair. A proctopexy is my go-to procedure these days. This is a patient who otherwise might even be considered for a perineal procedure, just given her age and other comorbidities. But I have been so happy with the speed and safety of a proctopexy. That is my go-to for even patients in the past that I used to do an Altmyer.

The surgeon opens the area over the sacral promontory and performs a posterior and right lateral total mesorectal excision (TME) dissection. The dissection reveals the crackly fascia and smooth glistening tissue, navigating through scarring and redundancy in the retroperitoneum. The robotic arms are adjusted to optimize angles, with scissors positioned in arm four, occasionally requiring port hopping to avoid contact with the iliac vessels.

The surgeon is positioned far down posteriorly, performing right lateral dissection while preserving the right lateral stalks. The presacral veins are visible, and the surgeon carefully maintains hemostasis while lifting the mesorectum off the fascia of the levator muscles. The camera is adjusted to view the deep pelvis.

The surgeon navigates anteriorly, identifying the rectovaginal septum in a patient who has had a hysterectomy. The white tissue of the vagina is visible, and the surgeon uses tactile feedback to ensure proper plane identification, preparing to dissect lower while being cautious of nearby vessels.

The surgeon dissects the plane for ventral metro rectopexy, identifying the anal coccygeal ligament. The sacral promontory is cleaned of nerves and vessels, with the surgeon taking extra bites on the mesorectum for security. Arm number one holds the bowel on tension while arms two and four are used for tying, despite a suture break.

The surgeon takes a second bite using silk sutures, performing a unilateral pexy on one side to avoid kinking the rectosigmoid junction. The peritoneal opening along the anterior and right lateral mesorectum is closed using a 3-0 V-Loc suture, preventing the small bowel from descending into the posteriorly dissected space.

These cases are taking me about forty-five minutes in total these days with about thirty minutes of consult time. I'm almost exclusively doing these as outpatient procedures. I've been very happy with it. Again, these cases can absolutely be done laparoscopically if you're comfortable with your suturing and probably the same amount of time. and I really feel happy and comfortable with this procedure for my prolapse patients. I no longer will do a resection. Although the Frickman procedure, the resection rectopexy was my standard for many years of my career, I have converted to exclusively doing the pexy. I don't use mesh. I am extremely happy with my results. There are definitely many ways to do it. This is simply mine.
The surgeon inspects the pexy site, ensuring satisfactory tension and checking for any serosal injuries on the bowel, marking the conclusion of the operation.
